title: Add proper database migration framework (replace synchronize: true)
description:
CRITICAL: Heimdall currently uses Sequelize synchronize: true in production. No migration history, no rollback, column renames/removes impossible, data migrations impossible, production deployments risk data loss.

Replace with drizzle-kit:
- drizzle-kit pull --init to baseline existing schema
- drizzle-kit generate for schema diffs → SQL migration files
- drizzle-kit migrate to apply migrations sequentially
- Migration files committed to git (auditable history)
- CI checks that migrations are up to date

This is the FIRST thing to do in the Drizzle migration — it establishes the safety net for all subsequent schema changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] drizzle-kit pull --init generates baseline from existing DB
- [ ] synchronize: true removed from database.module.ts
- [ ] drizzle-kit generate produces migration SQL files
- [ ] drizzle-kit migrate applies migrations sequentially
- [ ] Migration files committed to git in drizzle/ directory
- [ ] Rollback tested (drizzle-kit has no native rollback — document manual process)
- [ ] CI validates migrations are current

title: Fix critical auth security: httpOnly cookies, JWT storage, SQL injection
description:
Immediate security fixes:
1. Set httpOnly: true on OAuth callback cookies (authn.controller.ts setSessionCookies)
2. Move JWT from localStorage to httpOnly cookie (requires frontend auth store change)
3. Fix SQL injection in evaluations.service.ts — searchFields from query params interpolated into Sequelize.literal/Op.iRegexp
4. Fix API_KEY_SECRET empty string fallback
5. Add auth guard to Tenable proxy endpoints
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] OAuth cookies have httpOnly: true
- [ ] JWT stored in httpOnly cookie, not localStorage
- [ ] SQL injection vectors in evaluations.service.ts fixed
- [ ] API_KEY_SECRET throws error if unset (no empty string fallback)
- [ ] Tenable proxy endpoints have JWT auth guard
- [ ] XSS cannot steal auth tokens

Auto-create users on first SAML login (same pattern as OIDC).
External group sync from SAML attributes.

title: Migrate Passport.js → better-auth (with Drizzle adapter)
description:
Migrate from 8 hand-rolled Passport.js strategies to better-auth. Sequelize blocker removed by Drizzle migration (izw.1+izw.2).

better-auth provides as plugins:
- Email/password (replaces passport-local)
- OAuth providers: GitHub, GitLab, Google, 30+ more (replaces passport-github, passport-gitlab2, passport-google-oauth)
- OIDC/SSO (replaces @govtechsg/passport-openidconnect for Okta + generic OIDC)
- MFA/2FA: TOTP + backup codes (NEW capability)
- Session management: database-backed, list/revoke individual sessions (NEW)
- Password reset: email-based flow (NEW)
- Rate limiting on all auth endpoints (NEW)
- Account lockout after N failures (NEW)

Files replaced:
- 9 strategy files in apps/backend/src/authn/
- authn.service.ts, authn.controller.ts, authn.module.ts
- 6 guard files in apps/backend/src/guards/
- token/token.providers.ts

Files kept:
- casl/casl-ability.factory.ts (CASL ABAC stays — better-auth RBAC supplements it)
- authz/ (authorization layer stays)

NestJS integration: @thallesp/nestjs-better-auth
Drizzle adapter: built-in (better-auth supports Drizzle natively)

LDAP gap: better-auth has community LDAP plugin. May need enhancement for Heimdall's SSL/TLS/search filter config. Evaluate during implementation.

title: Add unique constraints on join tables
description:
Three join/association tables lack unique constraints:
1. GroupUsers: no unique on (groupId, userId) — allows same user added to same group multiple times
2. GroupEvaluations: no unique on (groupId, evaluationId) — allows same eval shared to same group multiple times
3. EvaluationTags: no unique on (evaluationId, value) — allows duplicate tags

App prevents duplicates implicitly but DB doesn't enforce it. Duplicate join records could cause permission escalation bugs (user appears in group twice with different roles).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] UNIQUE(groupId, userId) on GroupUsers
- [ ] UNIQUE(groupId, evaluationId) on GroupEvaluations
- [ ] UNIQUE(evaluationId, value) on EvaluationTags
- [ ] Existing duplicate rows cleaned up before constraints added
- [ ] Migration tested

title: Add indexes on all FK columns (10+ missing)
description:
Sequelize auto-sync creates FK columns but does NOT create indexes. The following FK columns almost certainly lack indexes:
- Evaluations.userId, Evaluations.groupId
- EvaluationTags.evaluationId
- GroupUsers.groupId, GroupUsers.userId
- GroupEvaluations.groupId, GroupEvaluations.evaluationId
- ApiKeys.userId, ApiKeys.groupId

The evaluations service performs complex JOINs across all these tables. Without indexes, queries degrade at scale.

Also add index on Users.email (used in login lookups and SQL literals).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Index on every FK column listed above
- [ ] Index on Users.email
- [ ] Query performance tested with EXPLAIN ANALYZE before/after
- [ ] Migration file generated via drizzle-kit

title: Add FK constraints to all relationships (3 missing)
description:
Three relationships explicitly disable FK constraints with constraints: false:
1. Evaluation.userId → Users.id (evaluation.model.ts)
2. ApiKey.userId → Users.id (apikey.model.ts)
3. ApiKey.groupId → Groups.id (apikey.model.ts)

Also Evaluation.groupId is declared as @ForeignKey but likely has no DB-level constraint.

Risk: Deleting a User does NOT cascade to their Evaluations or ApiKeys at DB level. Orphaned rows possible. App handles cleanup in code (users.service.ts remove()) but crash mid-deletion leaves orphans.

Fix: Add proper FK constraints with ON DELETE CASCADE or ON DELETE SET NULL as appropriate. Test with existing data — verify no orphans exist first.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Evaluation.userId has FK constraint to Users.id
- [ ] ApiKey.userId has FK constraint to Users.id
- [ ] ApiKey.groupId has FK constraint to Groups.id
- [ ] Evaluation.groupId has FK constraint to Groups.id
- [ ] CASCADE/SET NULL behavior defined for each FK
- [ ] Existing orphan rows cleaned up before constraints added
- [ ] Migration tested against production-like data

description:

- Backup codes: 10 single-use codes generated on setup

Packages: otplib, qrcode

title: Add refresh token rotation + session management
description:
Replace the current 60-second JWT with proper access+refresh token pair:
- Access token: short-lived (15 minutes)
- Refresh token: long-lived (7 days), stored in httpOnly cookie, rotated on use
- New Sessions table: userId, refreshToken (hashed), expiresAt, deviceInfo, ipAddress
- POST /authn/refresh — rotate tokens
- GET /users/:id/sessions — list active sessions
- DELETE /users/:id/sessions/:sessionId — revoke specific session
- Failed login tracking: counter + lockout after 5 failures (15 min cooldown)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Access token 15 min, refresh token 7 days
- [ ] Refresh token rotation on use
- [ ] Sessions table with device/IP tracking
- [ ] Users can list and revoke individual sessions
- [ ] Failed login counter with lockout
- [ ] Rate limiting on all auth endpoints (not just /authn/login)
close_reason: SUPERSEDED: better-auth provides session management, refresh tokens, rate limiting, account lockout.

title: Backend Modernization: Drizzle + Zod + Auth Security
description:
Modernize Heimdall2 backend: Sequelize → Drizzle, class-validator → Zod, Passport.js → better-auth, add OpenAPI, fix auth security gaps, full database 3NF rebuild.

## Key Decisions
- ORM: Sequelize → Drizzle (first-party Zod integration, no Rust binary)
- Validation: class-validator → Zod (shared frontend+backend via drizzle-zod)
- Auth: Passport.js → better-auth (Sequelize blocker removed by Drizzle migration)
- Authorization: Keep CASL (ABAC — better-auth's RBAC supplements, doesn't replace)
- API docs: OpenAPI 3.0 via nestjs-zod + @nestjs/swagger
- Database: Full 3NF rebuild with drizzle-kit migrations

## Motivation
- 6 critical security vulnerabilities (httpOnly, JWT storage, no MFA, SQL injection, API keys never expire, no password reset)
- synchronize: true in production (no migrations)
- 8 hand-rolled Passport.js strategies → better-auth handles OAuth, OIDC, SAML, MFA as plugins
- Triple-defined schemas → Drizzle+Zod eliminates
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Sequelize fully removed, Drizzle as sole ORM
- [ ] All 7 tables have FK constraints, indexes, unique constraints
- [ ] drizzle-kit migrations working (no synchronize: true)
- [ ] All 23 DTOs use Zod via nestjs-zod
- [ ] OpenAPI 3.0 spec auto-generated and accessible at /api/docs
- [ ] OAuth cookies are httpOnly
- [ ] JWT not stored in localStorage
- [ ] Refresh token rotation implemented
- [ ] 2FA (TOTP) available for all users
- [ ] Rate limiting on all auth endpoints
- [ ] Failed login tracking + account lockout
- [ ] API keys have TTL + rotation endpoint
- [ ] SQL injection vectors fixed
- [ ] Zero critical/high security vulnerabilities

- Header format: { text: 'Name', value: 'field' } → { title: 'Name', key: 'field' }
- :sort-by.sync → v-model:sort-by (value is now Array of objects)
- header-props → REMOVED (use slots)
- footer-props → REMOVED (use items-per-page-options prop)
- mobile-breakpoint → REMOVED
- expanded-item slot → expanded-row slot
- item-key → items need id field, or use item-value prop
- All .sync modifiers → v-model:propName

11 files with v-data-table (29 occurrences):
1. SampleList.vue — custom headers, footer, .sync for sort/page/options (MOST COMPLEX)
2. UserManagement.vue — user CRUD table with $set
3. LoadFileList.vue — evaluation list with pagination, 6x .sync
4. ControlTable.vue — main results table
5. Statistics.vue — admin stats table
6-11. Various simpler table usages

Each file needs individual manual review and restructuring.

title: Phase 2: Vuetify 2→3 template migration (API-by-API)
description:

title: Vue 2/Vuetify 2 → Vue 3/Vuetify 3 Migration
description:
Migrate Heimdall2 frontend from Vue 2.7.16 + Vuetify 2.3.9 to Vue 3 + Vuetify 3.

## Motivation
- Vue 2 EOL since Dec 2023 (2+ years without security patches)
- Vuetify 2 EOL since Jan 2025 (1+ year)
- Active unpatched CVEs: CVE-2025-8083 (CVSS 8.6 prototype pollution), CVE-2025-8082 (XSS)
- vue-class-component and vue-property-decorator are abandoned (no Vue 3 version)

## Scope
- 96 Vue SFC components (all class-based with decorators)
- 786 Vuetify component usages across 71 files
- 16 Vuex store modules with vuex-module-decorators
- 6 class-based mixins
- 7 third-party Vue 2-only packages

## Key Constraint
Vuetify 2 CANNOT run on Vue 3 even with @vue/compat. Must migrate Vuetify simultaneously.

## Research (completed 2026-03-12)
Full docs: ~/.claude/projects/-Users-alippold-github-mitre-heimdall2/vue3-migration-research/

## Previous Attempts
- feature/vue3-migration (Oct 2025) — migrated main.ts, router.ts, store.ts only
- feature/nuxt-ui-modernization (late 2025) — ground-up rewrite with Nuxt UI
design:
Two approaches evaluated — team to choose before starting:

Plan A: Incremental via @vue/compat (10-14 weeks, 400-560 hours)
- Vue 3 compat mode keeps class components working during transition
- Vuetify 2→3 template changes happen in one pass (weeks 3-6)
- Class components converted incrementally afterward
- App stays functional throughout; can ship intermediate states

Plan B: Clean frontend rewrite (12-16 weeks, 480-640 hours)
- New frontend-v3/ directory with Vue 3 + Vuetify 3 + Vite + Composition API
- Port page-by-page, rewriting as you go
- Old and new run in parallel during transition
- Clean codebase, no compat baggage

Recommended: Plan A for minimal disruption; Plan B if team wants clean architecture
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] All 96 components render correctly on Vue 3 + Vuetify 3
- [ ] Zero @vue/compat warnings (compat mode fully removed)
- [ ] All CVEs resolved (Vuetify 2, @vue/cli-service, postcss, transitive deps)
- [ ] All existing tests pass on new framework versions
- [ ] Visual parity with current UI (verified page-by-page)
- [ ] No class-component or vue-property-decorator imports remain
- [ ] No vuex-module-decorators imports remain
- [ ] Build uses Vite (not @vue/cli-service)
- [ ] Performance equal or better than Vue 2 baseline
notes:
User decision (2026-03-12): Use Pinia (not Vuex 4), Composition API with <script setup>, and composables pattern for shared logic.

## Architecture Decisions (2026-03-12)
- Primary reference: vitify-next (MIT, ECharts, Vuetify 4, Pinia, strict TS)
- Cherry-pick DX from: logue/vite-vuetify-ts-starter (ESLint flat, OxLint, Stylelint, Husky)
- Cherry-pick dashboard cards from: Materio Free (ApexCharts reactive theme, card components)
- Skip: Able Pro (mediocre code quality, not worth $69-399)
- Charting: Move to Apache ECharts (aligns with Issue #210 plan)
- State: Pinia Composition API style (eliminates vuex-module-decorators)
- Components: <script setup lang="ts"> with auto-imports
- Icons: @mdi/js SVG paths (eliminates 7MB webfont)
- Build: Vite 7 with 8-plugin stack
- Testing: Vitest + Playwright + @pinia/testing
- Linting: ESLint flat + OxLint + Stylelint + Husky
- Full analysis: .beads/vue3-migration-research/06-template-comparison-and-recommendation.md

## Strategy Change (2026-03-12)
REJECTED: Incremental @vue/compat approach (proven to fail — vuex-module-decorators incompatible, Vuetify 2 templates must all change at once)
ADOPTED: Clean scaffold approach — new frontend with vitify-next patterns, port pages, Claude Code batch-converts components.
See: 08-migration-strategy-paths.md, 09-heimdall3-strategy.md

## Superseded Cards
- c72.3.1 (vue-apexcharts → vue3-apexcharts): Going straight to ECharts
- c72.3.2 (vuelidate → @vuelidate/core): Using VeeValidate 4 + @vee-validate/zod

## Board Structure Note (2026-03-12)
The Phase 2-5 parent cards contain WORK ITEMS (specific changes needed) but in the clean scaffold approach they are NOT executed sequentially as phases. Instead, when porting a PAGE, you do the relevant items from Phase 2 (Vuetify templates), Phase 3 (lib replacements), Phase 4 (stores), and Phase 5 (component conversion) ALL AT ONCE for that page.

Execution order is PAGE-BY-PAGE per the execution plan:
1. Scaffold (c72.1) — infrastructure only
2. Stores + Composables (c72.4 children) — foundation for all pages
3. MVP pages: Login, Landing, Results (doing c72.2/3/5 items as needed per page)
4. Remaining pages: Compare, Admin, Groups, Export, Upload readers
5. QA (c72.6) — final validation

description:
DoD environments use CAC (Common Access Card) and PIV smart cards for authentication. These present X.509 client certificates via TLS mutual authentication.

title: Fix evaluation pagination hack (20x over-fetch)
description:
evaluations.service.ts contains a documented pagination hack. Because eager-loaded JOINs inflate row counts (one evaluation with 3 group members returns 3 rows), the actual LIMIT is multiplied by hardcoded totalGroupMembers=20. Results are then sliced in JavaScript.

This means:
- Up to 20x more data fetched than needed
- Pagination offsets may be inaccurate
- Memory usage scales with group membership, not evaluation count

Fix with Drizzle: use subquery for pagination (SELECT ids first with proper LIMIT, then JOIN for full data). Or use Drizzle's distinct + proper offset/limit.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Pagination uses proper SQL (no 20x over-fetch)
- [ ] Offset/limit accurate regardless of group membership count
- [ ] Memory usage proportional to page size only
- [ ] Query performance tested with EXPLAIN ANALYZE
- [ ] All pagination tests pass

title: Fix redundant Evaluation.groupId (2NF violation)
description:
Evaluations table has BOTH:
1. Direct groupId FK column (set when uploaded via group API key)
2. Many-to-many to Groups via GroupEvaluations join table

The controller sets BOTH for API key uploads (line 166-173) — same relationship recorded twice. The groupId column captures 'uploaded by this group' while GroupEvaluations captures 'shared with these groups'.

Options:
A) Remove Evaluation.groupId, add 'uploadedVia' column to GroupEvaluations to distinguish the uploader group
B) Keep groupId but rename to uploadGroupId and document the semantic difference
C) Keep as-is but add documentation (least disruption)

Recommend Option A for proper normalization.

title: Evaluate Evaluation.data JSON blob (1NF violation)
description:
The data column stores ENTIRE HDF/InSpec results as a single JSON blob. This is the largest normalization violation — makes it impossible to:
- Query individual controls at DB level
- Index on fields within results
- Perform efficient partial updates
- Enforce referential integrity on nested structures

The service works around this by excluding 'data' from list queries (too expensive to load).

This is a DELIBERATE denormalization for simplicity. Normalizing it (separate tables for profiles, controls, results) would be a massive schema change affecting the entire data pipeline.

Options:
A) Full normalization into relational tables (huge effort, biggest payoff)
B) Use PostgreSQL JSONB with GIN indexes for queryability without normalization
C) Keep as-is, document as known limitation (least effort)
D) Hybrid: keep JSON blob but extract key fields as indexed columns (status counts, profile names)

Recommend Option B or D as pragmatic middle ground.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Decision documented: normalize, JSONB+indexes, keep as-is, or hybrid
- [ ] If JSONB: column type changed, GIN index added
- [ ] If hybrid: extracted columns defined and populated
- [ ] Query performance benchmarked for common access patterns
- [ ] Frontend data loading not degraded
